sections.title: The Three Cases
sections.explanation: Arabic has three grammatical cases that show the role a noun plays in a sentence. The nominative case (مرفوع) is marked with a damma (-ُ) and is used for subjects. The accusative case (منصوب) is marked with a fatha (-َ) and is used for objects. The genitive case (مجرور) is marked with a kasra (-ِ) and is used after prepositions and in possessive constructions (إضافة). These short vowel marks appear on the last letter of the noun. For example, the word كتاب (book) can appear as كِتابٌ (nominative — a book, as subject), كِتابًا (accusative — a book, as object), or كِتابٍ (genitive — a book, after a preposition). This system is called الإعراب (iʿrāb) and is one of the defining features of Arabic grammar.
sections.tip: Think of the cases as answering: WHO did it? (nominative/مرفوع), WHAT was affected? (accusative/منصوب), and connected to WHAT? (genitive/مجرور). The vowel marks -ُ -َ -ِ are your guide.

examples.en: The student came. — الطالبُ is nominative (subject), marked with damma -ُ
I read the book. — الكتابَ is accusative (object), marked with fatha -َ
I went to the school. — المدرسةِ is genitive (after preposition), marked with kasra -ِ
nominative, accusative, genitive — the three case names in Arabic

exercises.prompt: How many grammatical cases does Arabic have?
exercises.explanation: Arabic has three cases: nominative (مرفوع), accusative (منصوب), and genitive (مجرور). Each is marked by a different short vowel on the last letter of the noun.

exercises.prompt: Which short vowel marks the nominative case (مرفوع)?
exercises.explanation: The nominative case is marked with damma (-ُ). It appears on the last letter of the noun when it serves as the subject of the sentence.

exercises.prompt: In the sentence قرأتُ الكتابَ (I read the book), why does الكتاب have a fatha (-َ)?
exercises.explanation: الكتابَ is the direct object of the verb قرأتُ (I read). Direct objects take the accusative case (منصوب), which is marked with fatha (-َ).

sections.title: Tanwīn and Definite Marking
sections.explanation: The case endings have two forms depending on whether the noun is definite or indefinite. Indefinite nouns receive tanwīn (تنوين) — a doubled version of the case vowel that adds an '-n' sound: -ٌ (un), -ً (an), -ٍ (in). Definite nouns (those with the article ال or in an إضافة construction) receive the simple vowel without the 'n': -ُ (u), -َ (a), -ِ (i). For example, كِتابٌ means 'a book' (indefinite nominative with tanwīn), while الكِتابُ means 'the book' (definite nominative without tanwīn). Note that the accusative tanwīn -ً is usually written on an extra alif: كِتابًا. This alif is a spelling convention and is not pronounced separately.
sections.tip: Simple rule: if the noun has ال (the), use the plain vowel (-ُ -َ -ِ). If the noun has no ال and is indefinite, double the vowel to get tanwīn (-ٌ -ً -ٍ). The tanwīn adds an 'n' sound: kitābun, kitāban, kitābin.

examples.en: a book (indefinite, tanwīn -ٌ) / the book (definite, plain -ُ) — both nominative
a book (indefinite, tanwīn -ً) / the book (definite, plain -َ) — both accusative
a book (indefinite, tanwīn -ٍ) / the book (definite, plain -ِ) — both genitive
I saw a student. — indefinite accusative with tanwīn -ً and alif seat

options: A doubled case vowel on indefinite nouns that adds an '-n' sound
A mark that removes the case ending
exercises.explanation: Tanwīn is the doubling of a case vowel on indefinite nouns, producing an 'n' sound at the end: -ٌ (un), -ً (an), -ٍ (in). It only appears on indefinite nouns — nouns with ال do not take tanwīn.

exercises.prompt: Which form is correct for 'a book' as the subject of a sentence?
exercises.explanation: An indefinite subject takes tanwīn damma (-ٌ). كِتابٌ is correct. You never combine ال with tanwīn — a noun is either definite (ال + plain vowel) or indefinite (tanwīn).

exercises.prompt: Why is there an extra alif (ا) in كِتابًا?
options: It changes the meaning of the word
It is a spelling convention for the accusative tanwīn -ً
exercises.explanation: The accusative tanwīn -ً is conventionally written on a 'seat' alif (ا) at the end of the word: كِتابًا. This alif is a spelling rule, not a separate sound. The word is pronounced 'kitāban'.

exercises.prompt: How would you write 'the school' in the genitive case (after a preposition)?
exercises.explanation: المدرسة is definite (it has ال), so it takes the plain kasra (-ِ) for the genitive case: المدرسةِ. Tanwīn (-ٍ) would only be used on the indefinite form مدرسةٍ.

sections.title: When to Use Each Case
sections.explanation: The nominative case (مرفوع) is used for the subject of a verbal sentence (الفاعل), the subject and predicate of a nominal sentence (المبتدأ والخبر), and the agent in passive constructions (نائب الفاعل). The accusative case (منصوب) is used for the direct object (المفعول به), adverbs of time and place (ظرف الزمان والمكان), and after certain particles like إنَّ and أنَّ. The genitive case (مجرور) is used after prepositions (في، على، إلى، من، عن، بِ، لِ) and for the second noun in an إضافة (possessive) construction. For example: الطالبُ كتبَ الدرسَ في الدفترِ (The student wrote the lesson in the notebook) — الطالبُ is nominative (subject), الدرسَ is accusative (object), الدفترِ is genitive (after في).
sections.tip: A handy memory aid: prepositions ALWAYS trigger the genitive case. If you see في، على، إلى، من، عن، بِ، or لِ before a noun, that noun must be مجرور (genitive) with kasra or tanwīn kasra.

examples.en: The student wrote the lesson in the notebook. — subject (مرفوع), object (منصوب), after في (مجرور)
Indeed, knowledge is light. — إنَّ puts العلمَ in accusative; نورٌ is the predicate in nominative
I went from the house to the market. — both nouns are genitive after prepositions
the teacher's book — إضافة: المعلّمِ is genitive (second term of إضافة)

exercises.prompt: In the sentence كتبَ الولدُ الرسالةَ (The boy wrote the letter), what case is الرسالةَ in?
exercises.explanation: الرسالةَ is the direct object (المفعول به) of the verb كتبَ. Direct objects take the accusative case (منصوب), marked with fatha (-َ).

exercises.prompt: After the particle إنَّ, what case does the noun take?
exercises.explanation: The particle إنَّ (indeed/verily) puts its subject in the accusative case. So إنَّ العلمَ نورٌ — العلمَ is accusative with fatha.

exercises.prompt: What case does a noun take after a preposition like في (in) or على (on)?
exercises.explanation: Prepositions always govern the genitive case (مجرور). Any noun following في، على، إلى، من، عن، بِ، or لِ must be in the genitive, marked with kasra (-ِ) or tanwīn kasra (-ٍ).

exercises.prompt: In كتابُ المعلّمِ (the teacher's book), why is المعلّمِ in the genitive?
options: Because it is the subject of the sentence
Because it is the second noun in an إضافة (possessive) construction
Because it is the object of a verb
exercises.explanation: In an إضافة construction, the second noun (the possessor) always takes the genitive case. المعلّمِ is genitive because it is the possessor in كتابُ المعلّمِ (the teacher's book).

sections.title: Case in Dual and Sound Masculine Plural
sections.explanation: Regular singular nouns show case through short vowels on the last letter, but dual nouns and sound masculine plurals show case through changes in their endings. The dual ending is -انِ (-āni) in the nominative and -يْنِ (-ayni) in both the accusative and genitive. For example: معلّمانِ (two teachers, nominative) vs. معلّمَيْنِ (two teachers, accusative/genitive). The sound masculine plural ending is -ونَ (-ūna) in the nominative and -ينَ (-īna) in the accusative and genitive. For example: معلّمونَ (teachers, nominative) vs. معلّمينَ (teachers, accusative/genitive). The sound feminine plural (-ات) behaves like a regular singular: -اتٌ (nominative), -اتٍ (accusative and genitive — note: accusative and genitive are the same for this plural).
sections.tip: For the dual, remember: -انِ for the subject, -يْنِ for everything else. For the sound masculine plural: -ونَ for the subject, -ينَ for everything else. Both follow the same 'two-way' pattern.

examples.en: The two teachers came. — dual nominative ending -انِ
I saw the two teachers. — dual accusative ending -يْنِ
The teachers came. — sound masculine plural nominative ending -ونَ
I saw the teachers. — sound masculine plural accusative ending -ينَ
The (female) teachers came / I saw the (female) teachers. — feminine plural: -اتُ (nom.) / -اتِ (acc. & gen.)

exercises.prompt: What is the dual ending for a noun in the nominative case?
exercises.explanation: The dual nominative ending is -انِ (-āni). For example, معلّمانِ means 'two teachers' as the subject of a sentence. In the accusative and genitive, the ending changes to -يْنِ (-ayni).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the teachers' (masculine, as subject) using the sound masculine plural?
exercises.explanation: The sound masculine plural in the nominative (subject) takes the ending -ونَ: المعلّمونَ. The ending -ينَ (المعلّمينَ) is used for accusative and genitive.

exercises.prompt: In سلّمتُ على المهندسينَ (I greeted the engineers), why is the ending -ينَ and not -ونَ?
options: Because it follows the preposition على (genitive), and the sound masculine plural uses -ينَ for accusative and genitive
Because it is a broken plural
exercises.explanation: After the preposition على, the noun must be in the genitive case. The sound masculine plural uses -ينَ for both accusative and genitive, so المهندسينَ is correct here.

exercises.prompt: What is unusual about the sound feminine plural (-ات) in the accusative case?
options: It takes the ending -اتًا with an alif
Its accusative and genitive forms are the same: -اتٍ (indefinite) / -اتِ (definite)
It does not change at all
exercises.explanation: The sound feminine plural is unique: its accusative and genitive endings are identical. Both use kasra: -اتٍ (indefinite) or -اتِ (definite). Only the nominative is different: -اتٌ / -اتُ.

sections.title: Putting It All Together
sections.explanation: Let us analyze a complete sentence to see all three cases in action. Consider: سألَ الطالبُ المعلّمَ عن الدرسِ الجديدِ (The student asked the teacher about the new lesson). Here, الطالبُ is the subject (فاعل) and takes the nominative case with damma: الطالبُ. المعلّمَ is the direct object (مفعول به) and takes the accusative case with fatha: المعلّمَ. الدرسِ comes after the preposition عن, so it takes the genitive case with kasra: الدرسِ. The adjective الجديدِ agrees with its noun الدرسِ in case, so it is also genitive with kasra. This agreement rule is important: adjectives must match their noun in case, gender, number, and definiteness.
sections.tip: When in doubt about a noun's case, ask three questions: (1) Is it the doer of the action? → nominative. (2) Is it what the action is done to? → accusative. (3) Is it after a preposition or part of إضافة? → genitive. Adjectives simply copy the case of the noun they describe.

examples.ar: سألَ الطالبُ المعلّمَ عن الدرسِ الجديدِ
examples.en: The student asked the teacher about the new lesson. — three cases in one sentence
The boy read a useful book. — كتابًا مفيدًا: both noun and adjective are indefinite accusative
in the big city — both noun and adjective are definite genitive after في

exercises.prompt: In سألَ الطالبُ المعلّمَ عن الدرسِ, which word is in the accusative case?
exercises.explanation: المعلّمَ is the direct object of the verb سألَ (asked), so it takes the accusative case marked with fatha (-َ). الطالبُ is nominative (subject) and الدرسِ is genitive (after عن).

exercises.prompt: In قرأَ الولدُ كتابًا مفيدًا (The boy read a useful book), why does مفيدًا also have tanwīn fatha?
options: Because adjectives agree with their noun in case — كتابًا is accusative, so مفيدًا must also be accusative
Because all adjectives always take tanwīn fatha
exercises.explanation: Adjectives must agree with the noun they modify in case, gender, number, and definiteness. Since كتابًا is indefinite accusative, the adjective مفيدًا must also be indefinite accusative with tanwīn fatha.
